annotations.label: Free scalar field action

anchor.snippet: The action for the free relativistic scalar field theory is then

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of a field-theoretic action functional or scalar field theory.

annotations.label: Lagrangian density

anchor.snippet: is known as a Lagrangian density

annotations.note: No Lagrangian density / Lagrangian field theory is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quadratic action

anchor.snippet: This is an example of a quadratic action, since each of the terms is quadratic in the field

annotations.note: Though QuadraticForm exists, the physical concept of a quadratic action functional is not formalized.

annotations.label: Mass term

anchor.snippet: The term proportional to m 2 is sometimes known as a mass term

annotations.note: No mass term of a field-theory Lagrangian is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Klein–Gordon equation

anchor.snippet: This is the Klein–Gordon equation

annotations.note: A grep for Klein–Gordon found no occurrences in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Interacting (nonlinear) theory

anchor.snippet: Such a theory is sometimes said to be interacting, because the Euler–Lagrange equation is now nonlinear

annotations.note: Interacting field theory is not a Mathlib concept.

annotations.label: General interacting action

anchor.snippet: The action for the most general such theory is

annotations.note: No general interacting action functional is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Interacting Euler–Lagrange equation

anchor.snippet: The corresponding Euler–Lagrange equation of motion is now

annotations.note: A grep for Euler–Lagrange found no occurrences in Mathlib (no calculus of variations).

annotations.label: Mass dimension

anchor.snippet: This is most often termed the mass dimension of the quantity.

annotations.note: Mass/scaling dimension of fields is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Classical scaling dimension

anchor.snippet: The classical scaling dimension , or mass dimension, Δ , of φ describes the transformation of the field under a rescaling of coordinates

annotations.note: No classical scaling dimension under coordinate rescaling exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Scaling dimension of the field

anchor.snippet: This fixes the scaling dimension of the field φ to be

annotations.note: The derivation fixing the field's scaling dimension is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Scale-invariance condition

anchor.snippet: The condition for a scalar field theory to be scale invariant is then quite obvious

annotations.note: Scale invariance of a field theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dimensionless coupling condition

anchor.snippet: the only dimensionless parameter g n satisfies

annotations.note: Coupling-constant dimensional conditions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Massless φ4 in D=4

anchor.snippet: the only classically scale-invariant scalar field theory in D = 4 is the massless φ 4 theory

annotations.note: This classification of scale-invariant theories is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conformal transformation

anchor.snippet: is said to be conformal if the transformation satisfies

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes angle-preserving conformal maps (IsConformalMap / Conformal), related infrastructure but not the QFT metric-rescaling conformal transformation.

annotations.label: Scale-invariant implies conformal

anchor.snippet: In fact, the scale-invariant theories in the previous section are also conformally-invariant.

annotations.note: The scale-invariance-implies-conformal-invariance theorem for field theories is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: φ4 Lagrangian density

anchor.snippet: The Lagrangian density is

annotations.note: The φ⁴ Lagrangian density is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Internal Z2 symmetry

anchor.snippet: This is an example of an internal symmetry , in contrast to a space-time symmetry .

annotations.note: Internal symmetry of an action (the φ→−φ Z₂) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Positive m² single minimum

anchor.snippet: has a single minimum, at the origin.

annotations.note: This statement about the potential's minimum in field theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Double well and broken symmetry

anchor.snippet: This is known as a double well potential

annotations.note: The double-well potential and spontaneous symmetry breaking are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kink soliton solution

anchor.snippet: The φ 4 theory with a negative m 2 also has a kink solution, which is a canonical example of a soliton

annotations.note: A grep for soliton/kink found nothing in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Domain wall

anchor.snippet: this solution is called a domain wall

annotations.note: Domain walls are not a Mathlib concept.

annotations.label: Sine-Gordon theory

anchor.snippet: Another well-known example of a scalar field theory with kink solutions is the sine-Gordon theory.

annotations.note: A grep for sine-Gordon found no occurrences in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complex scalar field theory

anchor.snippet: In a complex scalar field theory, the scalar field takes values in the complex numbers

annotations.note: Complex scalar field theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complex scalar action

anchor.snippet: The action considered normally takes the form

annotations.note: The complex scalar action functional is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: U(1)/O(2) symmetry

anchor.snippet: This has a U(1) , equivalently O(2) symmetry

annotations.note: Mathlib has the circle group (U(1)) as Circle, but the claim that the complex scalar action enjoys this symmetry is not formalized.

annotations.label: Mexican hat and Goldstone boson

anchor.snippet: spontaneous symmetry breaking is found if m 2 is negative

annotations.note: A grep for Goldstone found nothing; the Mexican-hat/Goldstone-boson analysis is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two real fields decomposition

anchor.snippet: One can express the complex scalar field theory in terms of two real fields

annotations.note: Decomposing a complex field into two real fields in field theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: O(N)-invariant Lagrangian

anchor.snippet: The Lagrangian for an O ( N )-invariant scalar field theory is typically of the form

annotations.note: The O(N)-invariant Lagrangian is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complex vector SU(N) theory

anchor.snippet: The theory can also be expressed for complex vector fields

annotations.note: The complex-vector SU(N) field theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ginzburg–Landau theory

anchor.snippet: one obtains the Ginzburg–Landau theory of superconductors

annotations.note: The only 'Ginzburg' match is the unrelated Erdős–Ginzburg–Ziv combinatorics theorem; Ginzburg–Landau theory is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantization on Hilbert space

anchor.snippet: the fields, and all observables constructed from them, are replaced by quantum operators on a Hilbert space

annotations.note: Hilbert spaces exist in Mathlib, but field quantization (operator-valued fields) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Canonical commutation relations

anchor.snippet: their canonical commutation relations are given by

annotations.note: Canonical commutation relations for field operators are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Free quantum Hamiltonian

anchor.snippet: while the free Hamiltonian is, similarly to above,

annotations.note: The free quantum field Hamiltonian is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Momentum space fields

anchor.snippet: A spatial Fourier transform leads to momentum space fields

annotations.note: Mathlib has a general Fourier transform (Real.fourierIntegral), but its application to quantum field operators to obtain momentum-space fields is not formalized.

annotations.label: Creation and annihilation operators

anchor.snippet: which resolve to annihilation and creation operators

annotations.note: Creation and annihilation operators are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Operator commutation relations

anchor.snippet: These operators satisfy the commutation relations

annotations.note: Commutation relations for creation/annihilation operators are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bare vacuum

anchor.snippet: is identified as the bare vacuum

annotations.note: The bare vacuum state is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fock space

anchor.snippet: This construction is called Fock space

annotations.note: Fock space is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vacuum annihilated by Hamiltonian

anchor.snippet: The vacuum is annihilated by the Hamiltonian

annotations.note: This statement about the vacuum and Hamiltonian is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Interaction Hamiltonian

anchor.snippet: Interactions can be included by adding an interaction Hamiltonian.

annotations.note: The interaction Hamiltonian is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Green's functions via path integral

anchor.snippet: known as the n -particle Green's functions, are constructed by integrating over all possible fields

annotations.note: Feynman path integrals and n-point Green's functions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generating function

anchor.snippet: All of these Green's functions may be obtained by expanding the exponential

annotations.note: The QFT generating functional for Green's functions is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wick rotation to partition function

anchor.snippet: A Wick rotation may be applied to make time imaginary.

annotations.note: Wick rotation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Feynman rules

anchor.snippet: calculated using the following Feynman rules

annotations.note: Feynman rules / diagrams are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Renormalization

anchor.snippet: This is normally handled by renormalization

annotations.note: Renormalization is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Beta function

anchor.snippet: The dependence of a coupling constant g on the scale λ is encoded by a beta function

annotations.note: Mathlib's Beta function is the Euler beta integral, unrelated to the renormalization-group beta function described here.

annotations.label: One-loop beta function for φ4

anchor.snippet: The β -function at one loop (the first perturbative contribution) for the φ 4 theory is

annotations.note: The one-loop β-function of φ⁴ theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantum triviality

anchor.snippet: A quantum field theory is said to be trivial when the renormalized coupling

annotations.note: Quantum triviality is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Aizenman triviality theorem

anchor.snippet: Michael Aizenman proved that the theory is indeed trivial, for space-time dimension D ≥ 5

annotations.note: Aizenman's triviality theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.
